title: The June Gap: Why Construction Businesses Finish Strong on Paper and Tight on Cash
linkedin:
June billings look strong. The P&L will reflect the best numbers of the year. The bank account in mid-July often tells a different story.

This is the June gap in construction. It is not poor management, and it is not an unusual year. It is the structural outcome of how construction revenue works.

Two things drive it.

Retention. On most commercial contracts in Queensland and NSW, clients withhold five to ten percent of each progress claim until practical completion. That retention balance sits on the debtors ledger as a receivable, but it cannot be collected the way a normal invoice can. For a business with several active projects, the cumulative total in June is often significant, and most of it will not arrive in July.

Work In Progress. WIP is the value of completed work on active contracts that has not yet been claimed. A business billing behind its actual completion rate has funded that gap from its own cash, even while revenue looks healthy on paper.

Then July arrives. Progress claims from June typically settle mid to late July. Subcontractor invoices from May and June fall due in the same window. Superannuation for the April to June quarter is payable by 28 July.

All of that lands while the new financial year pipeline is still converting from quotes.

The most practical step a construction business can take before 30 June is to map that July position while there is still time to do something about it. A 13-week cash flow forecast that runs the project billing schedule against committed outflows through to September shows the gap clearly, before it arrives as a surprise.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#CashFlow #AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
facebook:
Construction businesses often finish June with their strongest numbers on paper and their tightest cash position in the bank.

It is not bad luck. Retentions held on active contracts, WIP sitting in jobs not yet billed, a cluster of July obligations landing before client payments do. This is the June gap, and it is baked into how construction billing works.

If you run a construction or trade business in Queensland or NSW, what does your July cash position look like right now? Have you mapped it yet?

New post on the ProfitPulse blog walks through the mechanics and what to do about it before 30 June. https://profit-pulse.com.au/the-june-gap-why-construction-businesses-finish-strong-on-paper-and-tight-on-cash

#CashFlow #AustralianBusiness
google:
Construction and trade businesses in Queensland, NSW, and Victoria: June is typically your strongest billing month. The bank account in mid-July often tells a different story.

Retention balances withheld on active commercial contracts, Work In Progress sitting in jobs not yet fully billed, and a cluster of July obligations that arrive before client payments do. This is the June gap, and it is built into the structure of how construction revenue works.

The businesses that manage it well are the ones that map their July cash position before they get there. A 13-week cash flow forecast that runs the project billing schedule against committed outflows, including subcontractor invoices, super for the April to June quarter due on 28 July, and the new year pipeline still converting from quotes, shows where the gap will land and how wide it will be.

That picture is worth building now, in the last fortnight of June, while there are still decisions to make.

ProfitPulse has published a post this week that walks through the retention mechanics, the WIP position, and how to plan the July cash position before it arrives as a surprise.

Read it here: https://profit-pulse.com.au/the-june-gap-why-construction-businesses-finish-strong-on-paper-and-tight-on-cash
